title: Quiz: Gate Set Tomography
description: Test your understanding of gate set tomography principles and analysis

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Gate Set Tomography over standard quantum process tomography?

options.text: GST requires fewer measurements
GST simultaneously characterizes gates and SPAM, requiring no calibrated reference
GST is faster to analyze computationally
GST only works for Clifford gates

questions.explanation: GST's key advantage is self-consistency: it simultaneously estimates gate operations and SPAM (state preparation and measurement) without assuming any of them are perfect. Standard process tomography assumes perfect state preparation and measurement, which introduces errors.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'germs' in GST?

options.text: To initialize the qubit in a known state
To amplify all possible gate errors when repeated
To perform error correction
To measure the qubit state

questions.explanation: Germs are short gate sequences designed so that when repeated many times, they amplify all possible error modes (both Hamiltonian and stochastic errors). A complete germ set ensures that every error parameter grows with sequence length, enabling accurate estimation.

questions.question: What is 'gauge freedom' in GST and why does it matter?

options.text: The freedom to choose which gates to characterize
Different mathematical representations that give identical physical predictions
The uncertainty in measurement results
The calibration of measurement equipment

questions.explanation: Gauge freedom means that many different mathematical gate set representations produce identical experimental predictions. This is because the gates always appear sandwiched between state preparation and measurement. Gauge optimization chooses a specific representation that best matches target gates for interpretability.

questions.question: In GST error analysis, what does the Hamiltonian part of the error generator represent?

options.text: Random, incoherent errors like depolarization
Coherent errors like over-rotations or axis errors
Measurement errors only
State preparation errors only

questions.explanation: The Hamiltonian (H) part of the error generator represents coherent errors - systematic, unitary deviations from the ideal gate. These include over-rotations (wrong angle), under-rotations, and axis errors (rotating around slightly wrong axis). These are correctable through recalibration.

questions.question: What role do fiducials play in GST?

options.text: They correct for readout errors
They create informationally complete preparations and measurements
They provide reference gates for calibration
They reduce the number of required circuits

questions.explanation: Fiducials are sequences applied before (preparation) and after (measurement) the germ sequences. They rotate the native preparation and measurement to create an informationally complete set of states and measurement bases, enabling full tomographic reconstruction.

questions.question: GST requires more measurements than randomized benchmarking but provides more detailed error information.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. GST requires thousands of different circuits (compared to hundreds for RB) because it must probe all error parameters. However, it provides complete process matrices and detailed error decomposition, whereas RB only provides average fidelity.

questions.question: Why are germ sequences repeated at multiple lengths (1, 2, 4, 8, ...)?

options.text: To average out random noise
To increase the signal-to-noise ratio
To amplify small errors so they become measurable
To test gate stability over time

questions.explanation: Repeating germs amplifies errors linearly (coherent) or quadratically (stochastic) with length. Small errors that are hard to detect in a single gate become clearly visible after many repetitions. This amplification is essential for achieving the precision GST is known for.

questions.question: When should you choose GST over randomized benchmarking?

options.text: For routine daily monitoring of gate quality
When you need to diagnose specific error mechanisms for calibration
When measurement time is very limited
When characterizing many qubits simultaneously

questions.explanation: GST is ideal when you need detailed error information - for example, determining whether errors are coherent (fixable by recalibration) or incoherent (requiring hardware improvements). For routine monitoring with limited time, RB is more practical.
